news_summary: Today's technology news highlights Europe's ambition to become a science superpower through innovation in microscopy and digital currency, while emphasizing the importance of national strategies for AI development to ensure sovereignty, privacy, and stability.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité technologique d'aujourd'hui met en avant l'ambition de l'Europe de devenir une superpuissance scientifique grâce à l'innovation dans les domaines de la microscopie et de la monnaie numérique, tout en soulignant l'importance des stratégies nationales en matière de développement de l'IA pour garantir la souveraineté, la protection de la vie privée et la stabilité.
news_summary_es: Las noticias tecnológicas de hoy destacan la ambición de Europa de convertirse en una superpotencia científica a través de la innovación en el campo de la microscopía y las monedas digitales, al tiempo que subrayan la importancia de las estrategias nacionales para el desarrollo de la inteligencia artificial con el fin de garantizar la soberanía, la privacidad y la estabilidad.

articles.title: Europe as science superpower: what it will take to rival the US and China
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 23 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01955-1Amid chaos in US science and geopolitical turmoil, Europe wants to position itself as a research haven — but questions about funding and innovation remain.
articles.content:
“Europe will always choose science,” Ursula von der Leyen announced in May 2025 in a landmark speech that kicked off a European Union-wide campaign to attract scientists fleeing the chaotic US research environment. The president of the European Commission, the EU’s executive branch in Brussels, didn’t explicitly mention US President Donald Trump, but she didn’t have to. Trump’s administration had in the preceding months frozen grant funding, pulled the United States from global scientific organizations and begun firing federal research staff.

Europe’s leaders hope to fill the vacuum left by chaos in the United States — and position the region as an alternative research superpower. The EU has allocated nearly €900 million (US$1 billion) to its Choose Europe initiative, which aims to make the region a magnet for researchers around the world, and individual European countries have launched some 100 complementary schemes.

Europe must seize the moment to lead on free and open science

A renewed focus on innovation means that the EU’s main pan-national research-funding programme, Horizon Europe, could receive its largest-ever increase for the next seven-year cycle, which will run from 2028 to 2034, taking the fund’s value up to €175 billion. The programme’s reach also looks set to grow. Many science powers elsewhere in the world — including Japan, India and Australia — are joining parts of it, or are considering doing so, raising the prospect of a global research network anchored in Europe.

But as a whole, research and development (R&D) funding in Europe still languishes behind that in China and the United States. The region has found it hard to translate its academic research prowess into innovation and economic benefits. The past decade has been marked by the shocks of the United Kingdom’s 2016 decision to leave the EU and Russia’s invasion of Ukraine in 2022. That ongoing war, combined with weaker US defence support, has prompted increases in funding for military research.

It is early days, but Europe’s efforts to step up in global scientific leadership have so far been “more psychological than a visible positive upswing”, says Helga Nowotny, a social scientist in Zurich, Switzerland, who was previously the president of the European Research Council (ERC) — a part of Horizon Europe that focuses on researcher-led science.

The US retreat is an opportunity for Europe, says Anna Rubartelli, a cell biologist at San Martino Hospital’s scientific institute in Genoa, Italy. “The reality is that we are like a Sleeping Beauty and maybe it’s time to wake up.”

State of the Union

On some measures, it would be a case of both waking up and catching up. US science policymakers have described the country as being in “a two-nation race” with China. Both countries each spend more than US$1 trillion a year on public and private research funding, whereas Europe invests just $750 billion (see ‘Science spending’).

Source: OECD/UNESCO

Research spending as a proportion of gross domestic product (GDP) follows a similar pattern: although several European countries rival the United States in the share of their economy devoted to R&D, as a whole Europe lags behind the United States and China (see ‘Europe’s diversity’).

Source: Elsevier/Scopus database; CWTS Leiden/OpenAlex; OECD/UNESCO

But Europe does compete with the two research giants in other ways. It is home to world-class scientific organizations, such as the European Molecular Biology Laboratory in Heidelberg, Germany, and CERN, the particle-physics lab near Geneva, Switzerland. Europe, comprising 42 countries excluding Russia and Belarus, produces more research articles than the United States does each year — as does the EU with its 27 member states — although both are outdone by China (see ‘Article output’). This follows population trends: almost 600 million people live in Europe, including 450 million in the EU. That is larger than the United States’ 350 million but smaller than China’s 1.4 billion.

Source: CWTS Leiden/OpenAlex

Europe stands shoulder to shoulder with the United States and China when considering the influence and impact of its research. European scientists have a larger share of research papers published in the Nature Index, a selection of influential journals covering the natural sciences, than their US counterparts — but a smaller one than researchers in China (see ‘Influential science’).

Source: Nature Index

Papers published by EU researchers garner, on average, more citations — a measure of academic impact — than do those by China-based authors, but less than those by US scientists, according to a report by publisher and analytics firm Elsevier, headquartered in Amsterdam (see go.nature.com/4ax4y2e).

The region has a distinctly global research outlook. As a percentage of their total output, scientists in EU countries enter more international collaborations than do researchers in China or the United States (see ‘International collaboration’). Although some of these projects involve EU nations collaborating with each other, the bloc is on a par with the United States when excluding papers that are solely intra-EU collaborations, and it outperforms the United States and China when it comes to the share of its output co-authored with authors in the Middle East, Latin America and Africa, according to data from the Scopus database.

Source: Elsevier/Scopus database/Paola Barr

Although China’s engagement with Africa is increasing, for example in health and infrastructure projects, EU member states together are larger holders of assets in the continent than either the United States or China, according to the United Nations. The EU and its member states are collectively also the world’s largest provider of overseas development funding, according to data from the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development. European institutions “have the infrastructure, the culture, for collaborating, and that is the real power of the EU”, says Paola Barr, a networks analyst at Elsevier and a co-author of the Elsevier report.

Europe’s research is becoming increasingly connected to both China and the United States, too, even as the two superpowers collaborate less with each other. Nearly 45% of China’s scholarly output that includes international collaborations was done with US researchers in 2015. However, that fell to 24% in 2025 — close to that of China’s collaboration with Europe, according to data supplied by Barr. The EU rivalling the United States as a collaborative partner with China was “unimaginable in 2000”, Barr says.

But for all Europe’s prowess and connections, it struggles to create economic growth from its research. One major weakness is how little research happens in businesses. Whereas industry performs around 77% of all R&D in China and the United States, it contributes just 66% in the EU. Venture-capital spending on science and technology is falling in the EU, according to the World Intellectual Property Organization. Only 3.6% of papers with EU authors are cited in patents, compared with 5.8% in China and 5.0% in the United States, according to Elsevier’s report.

In a high-profile 2024 report on European competitiveness, Mario Draghi, an economist and former prime minister of Italy, noted the lack of a European technology giant such as Apple or Google (see go.nature.com/4eccd2a). “Europe must profoundly refocus its collective efforts on closing the innovation gap with the US and China,” he wrote.

Expanding horizons

So what changes are in store for European science? The European Commission’s proposal to raise Horizon Europe funding to €175 billion for the next cycle would amount to an increase of almost 50% when accounting for inflation, and some want to go higher. The European Parliament and an influential expert group led by Manuel Heitor, Portugal’s former science minister who is now a policy researcher at the Technical University of Lisbon, want to push up the sum to at least €200 billion. But there could be a fight ahead because the EU member states, which together with the European Parliament decide the Horizon budget, “aren’t exactly keen to empty their purses into the lap of Europe”, says ERC president Maria Leptin, in Brussels. She also supports pushing for at least €200 billion. The European Council, which represents the EU member states, opened negotiations earlier this month proposing €168 billion, a 4% cut compared with the commission’s figure. The council and parliament aim to conclude negotiations by the end of the year.

Brexit tore apart European science — now the research rifts are healing

Separately, the governments of several European countries, which fund most of the region’s research, are increasing science funding in some areas. Noting the need for home-grown innovation to spur economic growth and guard national security, France, Germany and the United Kingdom are making multibillion-dollar investments in quantum science and technology, for example. Together, these are on a similar scale to US government funding for the field, although they trail behind China’s public spending on quantum science. And there are efforts to align Horizon spending with that of individual member states so that money is used efficiently.

Horizon Europe is also expanding to include a raft of nations that have never been members before. ‘Associate countries’, which pay into the programme so that they are eligible for research grants, have typically been the EU’s geographical neighbours, such as Switzerland, Iceland, Norway and the United Kingdom. (The latter became an associate member four years after it left the EU in 2020). But in 2023, New Zealand joined, followed by Canada in 2024, South Korea and Japan in 2025, and Australia this year. All five have only partial access, however: they can’t receive basic-science funds but can have grants from a Horizon Europe programme aimed at tackling societal challenges. India has also started talks about association.

The combined GDP of countries outside the EU that have joined or shown interest in joining Horizon Europe is now bigger than that of the 27 member states, says Andrea Renda, a social scientist and director of research at the Brussels-based think tank the Centre for European Policy Studies. “There’s half of the world that is associating itself to Horizon Europe,” he says. As well as benefiting science by pooling expertise from a wider range of places, the expansion is an enormous opportunity to build a coalition of like-minded “middle powers”, he says, that could provide a counterweight to the United States and China in research. Many countries were already looking to join before Trump’s election, but his administration’s actions have accelerated and encouraged efforts, says Renda. “The window of opportunity is now.”

Europe is ditching US tech — what does this mean for researchers?

Those actions also prompted the EU’s Choose Europe initiative. As well as paying for extra relocation costs for talented researchers, under the initiative, the ERC will issue 30 ‘super-grants’ a year, designed to entice world-class scientists to move to Europe. The grants are for up to seven years — longer than the ERC’s other grants and the same length as the longest ones offered by the US National Institutes of Health, the world’s largest funder of biomedical research.

As political intervention continues to plague US science, European leaders are keen to highlight the region’s openness, diversity and data-sharing principles. “Scientists need stability, long-term funding, and — most importantly — the freedom to follow evidence, not politics. Europe offers all of this,” says European Commission spokesperson Maciej Berestecki.
articles.reasoning: The article describes Europe's strategic efforts to become a global science superpower by increasing research funding, expanding international collaborations, and attracting top scientists. This has broad positive implications for global scientific progress, innovation, and economic growth, benefiting society at large. The article provides detailed context on funding programs, geopolitical shifts, and collaborative networks, demonstrating substantial substance and significance.
articles.personality_title: Europe aims to become a global science leader by boosting research and collaboration
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Europe sees a chance to become a top science region after problems in US research funding and politics created uncertainty for scientists. 

**What happened** – Since 2025, the European Union has launched a big campaign called Choose Europe, investing nearly €900 million to attract scientists from around the world. They plan to increase the Horizon Europe research budget to about €175 billion for 2028–2034, the largest ever. More countries, including Canada, Japan, and Australia, are joining this program to form a global research network. 

**Impact** – Europe already produces many scientific papers and has strong collaborations worldwide. But it invests less in business research and innovation than the US and China. The new funding and partnerships aim to close this gap and make Europe a more attractive place for top researchers. This could lead to more discoveries and stronger economies. 

**What's next step** – EU leaders will decide the final Horizon Europe budget by the end of 2026. They also plan to offer special long-term grants to attract world-class scientists. Several European countries are increasing investments in areas like quantum technology. Expanding the research network with new countries will continue, helping Europe build stronger global ties. 

**One-sentence takeaway** – Europe is increasing research funding and global partnerships to become a leading science hub and attract top talent worldwide.
articles.personality_title_fr: L'Europe vise à devenir un leader mondial de la science grâce à un financement et une collaboration renforcés
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L'Europe voit une opportunité de devenir une région scientifique de premier plan après des problèmes de financement et de politique scientifique aux États-Unis qui ont créé de l'incertitude pour les chercheurs.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Depuis 2025, l'Union européenne a lancé une grande campagne appelée Choose Europe, investissant près de 900 millions d'euros pour attirer des scientifiques du monde entier. Elle prévoit d'augmenter le budget du programme Horizon Europe à environ 175 milliards d'euros pour la période 2028-2034, un record. Plus de pays, dont le Canada, le Japon et l'Australie, rejoignent ce programme pour former un réseau mondial de recherche.

**Impact** – L'Europe produit déjà de nombreux articles scientifiques et entretient de solides collaborations internationales. Mais elle investit moins dans la recherche en entreprise et l'innovation que les États-Unis et la Chine. Ces nouveaux financements et partenariats visent à réduire cet écart et à rendre l'Europe plus attractive pour les meilleurs chercheurs, ce qui pourrait conduire à plus de découvertes et à une économie plus forte.

**Prochaine étape** – Les dirigeants de l'UE décideront du budget final d'Horizon Europe d'ici fin 2026. Ils prévoient aussi d'offrir des subventions spéciales à long terme pour attirer des scientifiques de classe mondiale. Plusieurs pays européens augmentent leurs investissements dans des domaines comme la technologie quantique. L'expansion du réseau de recherche avec de nouveaux pays va se poursuivre, renforçant les liens mondiaux de l'Europe.

**Résumé en une phrase** – L'Europe augmente ses financements et ses partenariats mondiaux pour devenir un centre scientifique de premier plan et attirer les meilleurs talents du monde entier.
articles.personality_title_es: Europa busca convertirse en líder mundial en ciencia mediante más financiación y colaboración
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Europa ve una oportunidad para convertirse en una región científica líder tras problemas en la financiación y la política científica en Estados Unidos que generaron incertidumbre para los investigadores.

**Qué pasó** – Desde 2025, la Unión Europea lanzó una gran campaña llamada Choose Europe, invirtiendo cerca de 900 millones de euros para atraer científicos de todo el mundo. Planea aumentar el presupuesto del programa Horizonte Europa a unos 175 mil millones de euros para 2028-2034, la cifra más alta hasta ahora. Más países, incluyendo Canadá, Japón y Australia, se están uniendo para formar una red global de investigación.

**Impacto** – Europa ya produce muchos artículos científicos y tiene fuertes colaboraciones internacionales. Pero invierte menos en investigación empresarial e innovación que Estados Unidos y China. Los nuevos fondos y asociaciones buscan cerrar esta brecha y hacer que Europa sea más atractiva para los mejores investigadores, lo que podría traer más descubrimientos y una economía más fuerte.

**Próximos pasos** – Los líderes de la UE decidirán el presupuesto final de Horizonte Europa a finales de 2026. También planean ofrecer subvenciones especiales a largo plazo para atraer científicos de clase mundial. Varios países europeos están aumentando inversiones en áreas como la tecnología cuántica. La expansión de la red de investigación con nuevos países continuará, fortaleciendo los lazos globales de Europa.

**Resumen en una frase** – Europa está aumentando la financiación y las asociaciones globales para convertirse en un centro científico líder y atraer a los mejores talentos del mundo.
articles.image_prompt: A vibrant, detailed painting of a large, glowing European star-shaped constellation in a night sky above a peaceful, diverse network of interconnected research institutions symbolized by stylized observatories and laboratories linked with radiant bridges, with gentle warm tones of gold, blue, and soft white, conveying unity, scientific collaboration, and Europe awakening as a global science superpower.

articles.title: Making samples one billion times bigger lets simple microscopes pinpoint amino acids
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 23 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01842-9Stretching protein samples in all directions pulls molecules farther apart, allowing them to be visualized using only light microscopy.
articles.content:
High-resolution images can be produced by increasing the size of a nerve cell before fluorescence-microscopy imaging.Credit: Arthur Chien/SPL

A technique that supersizes cells to reveal minute details has gone big — really big.

Using a polymer similar to those used in nappies to make them super-absorbent, scientists have expanded the volume of biological samples so they are one billion times bigger — 1,000-fold larger in each dimension. This level of expansion could inflate an individual cell to the size of a mouse brain and a US-dime-sized sample to the proportion of an Olympic swimming pool.

Researchers have used the technique to map the positions of amino acids (the building blocks of proteins) in proteins and small molecules called peptides using conventional light microscopes. The approach is outlined in a preprint posted on bioRxiv earlier this month1.

Previously, viewing molecules in such fine detail has typically been achieved by using costly and complicated techniques such as cryogenic electron microscopy (cryo-EM) and X-ray crystallography. “This is the democratization of structural biology,” says study co-author Silvio Rizzoli, a neuroscientist and imaging specialist at the University Medical Center Göttingen (UMG) in Germany.

“It’s really getting down to a ground-truth description of what a protein is,” adds co-author Helena Hu, a bioengineer at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) in Cambridge.

Expansion microscopy

The laws of physics limit the optical power of light microscopes: objects that are less than about 200 nanometres apart cannot be distinguished. ‘Super-resolution’ light-microscopy techniques, which usually rely on optical tricks and costly kit, have cut this resolution limit to below 10 nm.

In 2015, MIT neuroengineer Edward Boyden, a co-author of the study, and his colleagues invented an alternative way to get extremely high resolution using ordinary fluorescent light microscopes2. Using a swelling hydrogel, the researchers expanded tissue samples about fourfold in each direction. This moved cellular components away from one another, improving resolution.

Researchers have since adapted Boyden’s ‘expansion microscopy’ method to swell samples to greater volumes, but inflation has generally been limited to about a 20-fold increase in size.

To go bigger, the researchers developed a new hydrogel recipe in which samples were expanded multiple times. The team also used an expansion-microscopy approach called ONE microscopy3 to map protein structures with a light microscope. Purified proteins were bonded to the hydrogel and then broken apart using enzymes or heat, enabling proteins to be stretched apart without compromising their 3D shape.

Gigantic proteins

Using their 1,000-fold expansion method, the researchers labelled several of the nine individual amino acids that make up a peptide called mCLING. They then imaged the relative locations of the building blocks using a fluorescent microscope. These measurements were consistent with structures of mCLING derived from computational simulations.

The method also revealed the structure of a protein called a nanobody (a small version of an antibody), as well as the locations of some of its amino acids. Furthermore, the researchers used the technique to map the previously determined structure of green fluorescent protein (GFP), a biotechnology workhorse used to label other proteins.

Expansion microscopy opens the door to exploring more challenges

The researchers estimate that their model of GFP — reconstructed from thousands of snapshots — attained a resolution of around 1.2 nm, or 12 ångströms. This is far from a 1.9-Å structure of GFP available in a public repository and determined using X-ray crystallography, or the level of detail that scientists routinely achieve using cryo-EM.

But co-author Ali Shaib, a nanoscale specialist at UMG, says that methodological improvements have driven resolution below 10 Å for purified proteins, in unpublished work. Rizzoli notes that the team is now able to determine the shapes of proteins that are still inside cells at around 10 Å. “We are trying to make cryo-EM like structures from affordable optical microscopes,” Shaib says.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in microscopy that allows detailed visualization of proteins and amino acids using affordable light microscopes. This democratizes structural biology by making high-resolution imaging accessible beyond expensive, specialized equipment, potentially accelerating research and discovery in biology and medicine with broad societal benefits.
articles.personality_title: New method makes tiny proteins visible with simple microscopes
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Seeing very small parts of cells, like proteins and amino acids, usually needs expensive and complex machines. Scientists want an easier way to look at these tiny things.

**What happened** – Researchers developed a new method that makes biological samples 1,000 times bigger in each direction, making them one billion times larger in total. They use a special polymer, similar to ones in diapers, to stretch cells and proteins so regular light microscopes can see details as small as amino acids. This technique was tested on proteins like mCLING, nanobodies, and green fluorescent protein (GFP).

**Impact** – This approach allows scientists to use affordable and simple microscopes to see very small structures that usually require costly tools like cryo-electron microscopes. It helps more people study proteins and understand their shapes, which can speed up discoveries in biology and medicine. Although the resolution is not yet as detailed as some advanced methods, it is improving quickly.

**What's next step** – The research team plans to improve the method to see proteins inside cells with even higher detail, aiming to match the quality of expensive machines but at a much lower cost. This could make detailed protein studies more common and accessible worldwide.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have found a way to make tiny proteins visible with simple microscopes by making samples a billion times bigger, opening new possibilities for affordable biological research.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle méthode rend les protéines minuscules visibles avec des microscopes simples
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Observer des parties très petites des cellules, comme les protéines et les acides aminés, nécessite généralement des machines coûteuses et complexes. Les scientifiques cherchent un moyen plus simple d’examiner ces éléments minuscules.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont développé une nouvelle méthode qui agrandit les échantillons biologiques 1000 fois dans chaque direction, soit un milliard de fois en volume. Ils utilisent un polymère spécial, semblable à ceux des couches, pour étirer les cellules et les protéines afin que des microscopes lumineux ordinaires puissent voir des détails aussi petits que les acides aminés. Cette technique a été testée sur des protéines comme mCLING, des nanocorps et la protéine fluorescente verte (GFP).

**Impact** – Cette approche permet aux scientifiques d’utiliser des microscopes simples et abordables pour observer des structures très petites, habituellement visibles seulement avec des machines coûteuses comme les cryo-microscopes électroniques. Cela aide plus de personnes à étudier les protéines et à comprendre leurs formes, ce qui peut accélérer les découvertes en biologie et en médecine. Bien que la résolution ne soit pas encore aussi précise que certaines méthodes avancées, elle s’améliore rapidement.

**Étape suivante** – L’équipe de recherche prévoit d’améliorer la méthode pour voir les protéines à l’intérieur des cellules avec encore plus de détails, visant à égaler la qualité des machines coûteuses mais à un coût beaucoup plus faible. Cela pourrait rendre les études détaillées sur les protéines plus accessibles dans le monde entier.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les scientifiques ont trouvé un moyen de rendre visibles les protéines minuscules avec des microscopes simples en agrandissant les échantillons un milliard de fois, ouvrant de nouvelles possibilités pour la recherche biologique abordable.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo método hace visibles las proteínas diminutas con microscopios simples
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Ver partes muy pequeñas de las células, como proteínas y aminoácidos, normalmente requiere máquinas caras y complejas. Los científicos buscan una forma más fácil de observar estas cosas pequeñas.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores desarrollaron un método nuevo que agranda las muestras biológicas 1,000 veces en cada dirección, haciéndolas mil millones de veces más grandes en total. Usan un polímero especial, parecido a los que se usan en pañales, para estirar células y proteínas para que microscopios de luz comunes puedan ver detalles tan pequeños como aminoácidos. Esta técnica se probó en proteínas como mCLING, nanocuerpos y la proteína fluorescente verde (GFP).

**Impacto** – Este método permite a los científicos usar microscopios simples y económicos para ver estructuras muy pequeñas que normalmente requieren equipos caros como los microscopios electrónicos criogénicos. Esto ayuda a más personas a estudiar proteínas y entender sus formas, lo que puede acelerar descubrimientos en biología y medicina. Aunque la resolución aún no es tan detallada como algunos métodos avanzados, está mejorando rápidamente.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo planea mejorar el método para ver proteínas dentro de las células con mayor detalle, buscando igualar la calidad de máquinas costosas pero a mucho menor precio. Esto podría hacer que los estudios detallados de proteínas sean más accesibles en todo el mundo.

**Resumen en una frase** – Científicos encontraron una forma de hacer visibles las proteínas diminutas con microscopios simples al agrandar las muestras mil millones de veces, abriendo nuevas posibilidades para la investigación biológica accesible.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of an enormous translucent nerve cell gently expanding like a glowing balloon over a simple wooden table, with delicate strands symbolizing amino acids stretching visibly apart inside it, illuminated softly by a classic fluorescent microscope casting a subtle light.

articles.title: Press release - Digital euro: MEPs want to ensure sovereignty, privacy and financial stability
articles.summary: The digital euro would offer citizens and businesses a private, secure and innovative way to pay, while reducing reliance on non-EU providers.Committee on Economic and Monetary Affairs Source : © European Union, 2026 - EP
articles.content:
On Tuesday, the Economic and Monetary Affairs Committee adopted its position on the single currency package, consisting of three files. The one on the establishment of the digital euro was adopted by 43 votes to 14, with 1 abstention.

The digital euro would be a new, electronic form of money issued by the European Central Bank (ECB) and would work online and offline. Online payments would be processed through an account-based system, while offline payments would work directly via local storage devices. The offline functionality would be equivalent to using physical cash, as losing the device would mean losing the offline money with no refund possible.

Privacy

Privacy-by-design and privacy-by-default principles would be built into the digital euro. Cutting-edge technologies, such as “zero-knowledge proofs”, would allow transactions to be verified without exposing personal data, which would be processed only to the extent strictly necessary for the system to function. The ECB would not have access to personal identification data.

Distribution model

All payment service providers (PSPs), including banks, e-money providers, post offices, and regulated crypto-asset providers, could distribute the digital euro across the EU. Most businesses would be required to accept it. Exceptions would apply to the self-employed, and small and micro enterprises that do not accept other digital payments.

Temporary refusals, such as during a power outage, would also be allowed under specific conditions. Visitors, tourists and, in some cases, people living outside the euro area would also be able to use it.

Fees and charges

Basic services, such as opening an account, holding and managing funds, and getting at least one payment instrument, would be free of charge. PSPs could charge for extra services, with the exception of account maintenance inactivity penalties or service bundling. Fees for merchant and inter-provider would be capped, while offline payments would be entirely fee-free.

Financial stability and holding limits

To protect the financial system, there would be a cap on how many digital euros any individual could hold. MEPs proposed the EU ceiling should be set by the Commission, based on ECB recommendations, and reviewed at least every two years. MEPs want the Parliament to have full decision-making powers in this process.

Businesses would not be allowed to hold digital euros, except to accumulate incoming payments for up to 24 hours. Crucially, the digital euro would not earn or cost any interest.

Seamless launch and the ECB's role

MEPs want to ensure that the ECB's role would be kept separate from its monetary policy functions. Before the launch, the ECB should finalise a rulebook, build the infrastructure, run real-life pilot tests, and iron out liability rules with particular attention to offline risks, like double-spending. Once authorised, a roll-out period of at least 24 months would follow, giving banks, providers, and users time to prepare. Governments and providers would also run awareness campaigns.

The single currency package

A second file on the provision of digital euro services by payment services providers incorporated in member states whose currency is not the euro, adopted by 43 votes to 9, with 6 abstentions, would allow banks and PSPs from non-euro EU countries to distribute the digital euro, subject to the same rules, while the ECB would retain the power to restrict access and use. Non-euro EU member states would also need to appoint a national authority to monitor any impact on their own currency.

A third file, on legal tender of euro banknotes and coins, adopted by 46 votes to 4, with 8 abstentions, would oblige euro area countries to keep cash accessible and plan for digital payment disruptions. Businesses would not be allowed to ban cash through "no cash" signs or standard contract terms. Member states would also need to check cash availability regularly, with special attention to vulnerable groups, such as the elderly, low-income individuals, and the unbanked.

Quote

Rapporteur Fernando Navarrete Rojas (EPP, ES) said: “With the single currency package, we are protecting citizens’ freedom to choose how they pay. We are strengthening access to and acceptance of cash, while making central bank money available in digital form. The digital euro will complement cash, never replace it. No one should be forced away from cash, and no one should be left without a secure, resilient and genuinely European digital payment option.

“Europe does not have to choose between the digital euro and successful private payment solutions. We need both to work together. The agreement rightly recognises the dual approach: existing standards and infrastructure should be reused wherever possible. This will allow European payment solutions to connect to a common acceptance infrastructure and become interoperable across borders.

“The agreement also ensures that privacy will be built into the digital euro from the outset. Europeans will gain a secure digital payment option while remaining in control of both their money and their personal data.”

Next steps

The negotiating mandates for the three texts will be announced at the start of the July plenary session. The final legislation will have to be negotiated with the Council before coming into force.
articles.reasoning: The article describes the European Parliament's adoption of a comprehensive framework for the digital euro, a central bank digital currency designed to enhance privacy, financial stability, and sovereignty in digital payments across the EU. This initiative has broad societal impact by providing a secure, private, and accessible digital payment option for all citizens and businesses, while maintaining cash accessibility and protecting vulnerable groups. The detailed provisions on privacy-by-design, fee structures, distribution, and safeguards demonstrate a substantive and meaningful advancement in digital financial infrastructure with significant public benefits.
articles.personality_title: European Parliament approves plan for a secure and private digital euro
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The European Union is creating a digital version of the euro, called the digital euro, to offer people a new way to pay using electronic money. This digital euro will be managed by the European Central Bank (ECB) and aims to work alongside regular cash.

**What happened** – On Tuesday, the European Parliament’s Economic and Monetary Affairs Committee voted to approve rules for the digital euro. The plan passed with 43 votes in favor, 14 against, and 1 abstention. The digital euro will work both online and offline, with payments processed through accounts or directly stored on devices, similar to cash. Privacy is a big focus, using special technology to keep personal data safe. Many types of payment providers, like banks and post offices, will help distribute the digital euro across the EU.

**Impact** – This decision is important because it creates a secure and private way for Europeans to pay digitally without relying on companies outside the EU. It also protects people’s privacy by design, meaning personal details won’t be shared unnecessarily. Basic services with the digital euro will be free, and offline payments won’t have fees. The digital euro will not replace cash but will work alongside it, ensuring everyone can still use traditional money. Limits on how much digital euro a person can hold will help keep the financial system stable.

**What's next step** – Before the digital euro is launched, the ECB will finish technical preparations and run tests to make sure everything works well and safely. The rollout will take at least two years, allowing banks, businesses, and users to get ready. Governments and providers will also inform the public about how to use the new digital euro. The European Parliament will negotiate final laws with other EU bodies in the coming months.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The European Parliament has set clear rules for a safe, private, and widely accessible digital euro that will work alongside cash and protect users’ data and financial stability.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Parlement européen adopte un plan pour un euro numérique sécurisé et privé
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L’Union européenne crée une version numérique de l’euro, appelée euro numérique, pour offrir aux citoyens un nouveau moyen de paiement électronique. Cet euro numérique sera géré par la Banque centrale européenne (BCE) et fonctionnera en complément de l’argent liquide.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Mardi, la commission des affaires économiques et monétaires du Parlement européen a approuvé des règles pour l’euro numérique. Le plan a été adopté par 43 voix pour, 14 contre et 1 abstention. L’euro numérique fonctionnera en ligne et hors ligne, avec des paiements traités via des comptes ou stockés directement sur des appareils, comme de l’argent liquide. La confidentialité est une priorité, grâce à des technologies spéciales protégeant les données personnelles. Différents prestataires de paiement, comme les banques et les bureaux de poste, distribueront l’euro numérique dans toute l’UE.

**Impact** – Cette décision est importante car elle offre aux Européens un moyen de paiement numérique sûr et privé, sans dépendre d’entreprises hors UE. Elle protège aussi la vie privée dès la conception, ce qui signifie que les données personnelles ne seront pas partagées inutilement. Les services de base avec l’euro numérique seront gratuits, et les paiements hors ligne sans frais. L’euro numérique ne remplacera pas l’argent liquide, mais fonctionnera à ses côtés, garantissant l’accès à tous. Des plafonds sur la détention d’euros numériques aideront à préserver la stabilité financière.

**Prochaine étape** – Avant le lancement, la BCE finalisera les préparatifs techniques et effectuera des tests pour assurer la sécurité et le bon fonctionnement. Le déploiement durera au moins deux ans, pour permettre aux banques, entreprises et utilisateurs de se préparer. Les gouvernements et prestataires informeront aussi le public sur l’utilisation de l’euro numérique. Le Parlement européen négociera ensuite la législation finale avec d’autres institutions de l’UE.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le Parlement européen a établi des règles claires pour un euro numérique sûr, privé et accessible, qui complétera l’argent liquide tout en protégeant les données et la stabilité financière.
articles.personality_title_es: El Parlamento Europeo aprueba un plan para un euro digital seguro y privado
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La Unión Europea está creando una versión digital del euro, llamada euro digital, para ofrecer a las personas una nueva forma de pago electrónico. Este euro digital será gestionado por el Banco Central Europeo (BCE) y funcionará junto al dinero en efectivo.

**Qué pasó** – El martes, el Comité de Asuntos Económicos y Monetarios del Parlamento Europeo aprobó las reglas para el euro digital. El plan fue aprobado con 43 votos a favor, 14 en contra y 1 abstención. El euro digital funcionará tanto en línea como fuera de línea, con pagos procesados a través de cuentas o almacenados directamente en dispositivos, similar al dinero en efectivo. La privacidad es una prioridad, utilizando tecnología especial para proteger los datos personales. Muchos proveedores de pago, como bancos y oficinas postales, ayudarán a distribuir el euro digital en toda la UE.

**Impacto** – Esta decisión es importante porque crea una forma segura y privada para que los europeos paguen digitalmente sin depender de empresas fuera de la UE. También protege la privacidad desde el diseño, lo que significa que los datos personales no se compartirán innecesariamente. Los servicios básicos con el euro digital serán gratuitos, y los pagos fuera de línea no tendrán tarifas. El euro digital no reemplazará al dinero en efectivo, sino que funcionará junto a él, asegurando que todos puedan seguir usando dinero tradicional. Los límites en la cantidad de euros digitales que una persona puede tener ayudarán a mantener la estabilidad financiera.

**Próximo paso** – Antes del lanzamiento, el BCE finalizará los preparativos técnicos y realizará pruebas para asegurar que todo funcione bien y de forma segura. El despliegue tomará al menos dos años, permitiendo que bancos, negocios y usuarios se preparen. Los gobiernos y proveedores también informarán al público sobre cómo usar el nuevo euro digital. El Parlamento Europeo negociará las leyes finales con otros organismos de la UE en los próximos meses.

**Conclusión en una frase** – El Parlamento Europeo ha establecido reglas claras para un euro digital seguro, privado y accesible que funcionará junto al dinero en efectivo y protegerá los datos y la estabilidad financiera.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a glowing, stylized digital euro coin gently cradled within an open, transparent network of interconnected hands made of light, symbolizing privacy, security, and European unity, set against a soft background of abstract circuit patterns and subtle outlines of diverse payment devices.

articles.title: Silicon Valley’s vision for global AI is flawed: each country needs its own blueprint
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 23 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01951-5From energy grids to language performance, emerging economies are exposing the limits of today’s artificial-intelligence strategy as it expands globally.
articles.content:
In April, South Africa withdrew its draft national artificial-intelligence policy after the document was found to have cited several fabricated academic references. These sources turned out to be AI ‘hallucinations’. The policy had been partially drafted using AI. The irony was hard to ignore: a framework designed to govern AI was undermined by the very technology it sought to regulate.

But beyond the hallucinated citations lies a deeper issue. The document reflected a broader assumption that is increasingly visible in AI policy worldwide: that global AI systems will be built by replicating and expanding the same computational and infrastructure model that has been pioneered in Silicon Valley for wealthy economies. Modern AI systems were developed under conditions of extraordinary abundance: cheap capital, plentiful energy, vast computing infrastructure and access to land and water for cooling. This has shaped the assumption that building ever larger models and increasing their scale is the way forward. But it is already running into physical constraints.

Nature’s message to South Africa’s next government: talk to your researchers

Data centres are notorious guzzlers of water and electricity. South Africa is a water-scarce country; until a year ago, it resorted to scheduled blackouts (referred to as load shedding) to manage its frequent electricity shortages. As a scientist based in South Africa, building various sizes of language models for emerging economies at Lelapa AI — a resource-efficient language AI company in Johannesburg — I see this disconnect as a fundamental strategy flaw.

High energy use is one barrier. The International Energy Agency, an intergovernmental body in Paris that shapes energy policies, projects that global electricity consumption for data centres will more than double between 2024 and 2030, reaching 945 terawatt-hours — approaching the entire annual electricity consumption of Japan in 2025. Even in infrastructure-rich regions, such as the United States and Ireland, electricity grids are struggling to keep up with the rapid growth of AI, prompting communities to push back against data-centre construction.

There are economic constraints, too. Hardware and running costs are high even in the wealthiest markets, and most AI companies aren’t yet making a profit from sales of AI products. To ‘super-scale’ their infrastructure quickly, some firms are relying on private credit — raising questions about whether the current pace of capital expenditure can be sustained by underlying revenue growth alone.

How South Africa can move on from power cuts

Nations are increasingly putting AI sovereignty — control over crucial AI infrastructure and systems — at the core of their security and growth agendas. Governments such as those of India, Saudi Arabia and countries in the European Union are investing in domestic compute infrastructure, cloud capacity and foundation AI models to achieve AI sovereignty and reduce dependence on foreign technology providers.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a significant and positive shift in global AI strategy, emphasizing the need for country-specific AI policies and infrastructure that address local resource constraints and promote AI sovereignty. This approach can lead to more sustainable, equitable, and efficient AI development worldwide, benefiting emerging economies and the global community.
articles.personality_title: Countries develop their own AI plans to fit local needs
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many countries are trying to use artificial intelligence (AI) to improve their economies and daily life. But current global AI systems mostly follow a model created in Silicon Valley, built for rich countries with lots of resources like energy and money.

**What happened** – South Africa recently withdrew its draft national AI policy after discovering it included fake academic references made by AI itself. This showed how difficult it is to create AI rules based on tools that are not perfectly reliable. More importantly, it revealed that South Africa’s energy and water shortages make the Silicon Valley AI model unrealistic for them.

**Impact** – This situation highlights that AI strategies need to be different in each country. For example, South Africa faces energy shortages and water scarcity, so building large data centers like those in wealthy countries is not practical or sustainable. Other countries like India and those in the European Union are investing in their own AI systems to reduce reliance on foreign technology and better fit their local needs.

**What's next step** – Emerging economies will likely create AI policies and technology plans tailored to their unique challenges, such as limited energy or infrastructure. This could lead to more efficient and fair AI development worldwide, where every country controls its own AI future.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Countries are realizing that AI must be built using plans that match their own resources and needs, not copied from wealthy nations’ models.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les pays développent leurs propres plans d’IA adaptés à leurs besoins locaux
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – De nombreux pays cherchent à utiliser l’intelligence artificielle (IA) pour améliorer leur économie et leur quotidien. Mais les systèmes d’IA actuels suivent surtout un modèle créé dans la Silicon Valley, conçu pour des pays riches disposant de beaucoup de ressources comme l’énergie et l’argent.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – L’Afrique du Sud a récemment retiré son projet de politique nationale sur l’IA après avoir découvert qu’il contenait de fausses références académiques générées par l’IA elle-même. Cela montre la difficulté de créer des règles pour l’IA basées sur des outils qui ne sont pas toujours fiables. Plus important encore, cela révèle que les pénuries d’énergie et d’eau en Afrique du Sud rendent le modèle de la Silicon Valley irréaliste pour ce pays.

**Impact** – Cette situation souligne que les stratégies d’IA doivent être différentes dans chaque pays. Par exemple, l’Afrique du Sud fait face à des pénuries d’énergie et d’eau, donc construire de grands centres de données comme dans les pays riches n’est ni pratique ni durable. D’autres pays comme l’Inde et certains pays de l’Union européenne investissent dans leurs propres systèmes d’IA pour réduire leur dépendance à la technologie étrangère et mieux répondre à leurs besoins locaux.

**Prochaine étape** – Les économies émergentes vont probablement créer des politiques et des plans technologiques d’IA adaptés à leurs défis uniques, comme des ressources limitées en énergie ou en infrastructures. Cela pourrait conduire à un développement de l’IA plus efficace et équitable dans le monde, où chaque pays contrôle son propre avenir en IA.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Les pays comprennent qu’il faut construire l’IA avec des plans qui correspondent à leurs propres ressources et besoins, et non en copiant les modèles des pays riches.
articles.personality_title_es: Los países desarrollan sus propios planes de IA según sus necesidades locales
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchos países intentan usar la inteligencia artificial (IA) para mejorar su economía y vida diaria. Pero los sistemas de IA actuales siguen principalmente un modelo creado en Silicon Valley, diseñado para países ricos con muchos recursos como energía y dinero.

**Qué pasó** – Sudáfrica retiró recientemente su proyecto de política nacional de IA después de descubrir que incluía referencias académicas falsas creadas por la propia IA. Esto mostró lo difícil que es crear reglas para la IA basadas en herramientas que no son totalmente confiables. Más importante aún, reveló que las limitaciones de energía y agua de Sudáfrica hacen que el modelo de Silicon Valley no sea realista para ellos.

**Impacto** – Esta situación destaca que las estrategias de IA deben ser diferentes en cada país. Por ejemplo, Sudáfrica enfrenta escasez de energía y agua, por lo que construir grandes centros de datos como en países ricos no es práctico ni sostenible. Otros países como India y miembros de la Unión Europea están invirtiendo en sus propios sistemas de IA para reducir la dependencia de tecnología extranjera y adaptarse mejor a sus necesidades locales.

**Próximo paso** – Las economías emergentes probablemente crearán políticas y planes tecnológicos de IA hechos a la medida de sus desafíos únicos, como recursos limitados de energía o infraestructura. Esto podría conducir a un desarrollo de IA más eficiente y justo en todo el mundo, donde cada país controle su propio futuro en IA.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Los países están entendiendo que la IA debe construirse con planes que se ajusten a sus propios recursos y necesidades, no copiando los modelos de las naciones ricas.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a stylized African baobab tree with glowing circuit-like roots spreading into the dry earth beneath a softly lit sky, surrounded by gentle silhouettes of water droplets and abstract cooling fans made from natural elements, symbolizing the blend of AI technology with South Africa’s unique environmental and energy challenges.
